questions.question: What property defines a linear code?

options.text: All codewords have the same length
The XOR of any two codewords is also a codeword
Each codeword has an even number of 1s

questions.explanation: A linear code is a vector subspace, meaning it's closed under addition. In binary codes, this means c₁ ⊕ c₂ (XOR) is also a codeword whenever c₁ and c₂ are codewords.

questions.question: For a systematic generator matrix G = [I_k | P], what do the first k bits of a codeword represent?
questions.explanation: In systematic form, the generator matrix starts with an identity matrix. This means the codeword c = m×G has the message m in its first k positions, making message extraction trivial.

questions.question: What is the relationship between G and H for a linear code?
questions.explanation: The generator matrix G and parity check matrix H satisfy G × H^T = 0 (mod 2). This ensures that all codewords generated by G have zero syndrome when checked by H.

questions.question: If C is an [n, k] linear code, what is the dimension of its dual C⊥?
questions.explanation: The dual code C⊥ has dimension n - k. This is because C⊥ is the null space of the generator matrix G, which has rank k, leaving n - k dimensions for the dual.

questions.question: What condition must classical codes C₁ and C₂ satisfy for CSS quantum code construction?
questions.explanation: The CSS construction requires C₂⊥ ⊆ C₁ (or equivalently C₂ ⊆ C₁⊥). This ensures that bit-flip and phase-flip error corrections are compatible with each other.

questions.question: A code C is called 'self-orthogonal' if:
questions.explanation: A code is self-orthogonal if every codeword is orthogonal to every codeword (including itself), meaning C ⊆ C⊥. This is equivalent to G × G^T = 0 (mod 2).

questions.question: The all-zeros vector (00...0) is always a codeword in any linear code.
questions.explanation: True. Linear codes are vector subspaces, which must contain the zero vector. Alternatively, c ⊕ c = 0 for any codeword c, so 0 is always in the code.
